narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, an LSAT score of 21, upper-middle family income, and plans to attend full-time, along with your school decile rankings from Year 1 and Year 3 both sitting at the lowest 10th percentile. The admissions model, trained on data from countless students' paths to the bar exam, predicted an 82.8% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try, which directly led to the rejection of your application since the school prioritizes those likely to pass and succeed in practice. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven assessment based on patterns from past students. The three biggest factors pushing the model toward this failure prediction were, in order of strongest influence, your Year 3 school decile ranking, your Year 1 school decile ranking, and your LSAT score—all of which worked against you by signaling higher risk compared to typical applicants. Your Year 3 decile in the bottom 10% (far below the average around the middle of the pack) had the most weight, as it strongly indicated challenges in sustaining strong academic performance heading into law school. Similarly, your Year 1 decile at the bottom 10% reinforced that early struggles often predict bar exam difficulties, outweighing other positives like your full-time commitment, which is the norm for 93% of students. Your LSAT score of 21, notably lower than the average of about 37, further tipped the scales toward failure by suggesting hurdles in the analytical skills key to bar success. While your GPA of 2.90 is a bit below the typical 3.21 and your family income is solid, they played smaller roles here. Compared to students who typically get approved, who often rank in higher deciles and score much stronger on the LSAT, your profile aligned more closely with those who historically struggled on the bar. This rejection stings, especially given your determination as a Hispanic man breaking into a tough field, but it's a signal to address these core academic markers. Next, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly, seeking targeted tutoring to improve your decile-level performance in any ongoing studies, and building a stronger narrative in reapplications that highlights your resilience and any upward trends. With focused improvements, you can shift these predictions in your favor and reapply stronger.
